items.title: ESA Greenlights Ramses Mission to Asteroid Apophis Ahead of 2029 Flyby
items.summary: The European Space Agency has approved full development of the Ramses spacecraft, which will rendezvous with asteroid Apophis before its historic close approach to Earth in April 2029.
items.content:
The European Space Agency has formally approved the Ramses mission, committing to launch a spacecraft toward asteroid 99942 Apophis in time to observe the 375-metre body before it passes within 32,000 kilometres of Earth — closer than geostationary satellites.

Mission planners at ESOC in Darmstadt say the probe must depart by spring 2028 to reach the asteroid ahead of the flyby. Ramses will carry a suite of cameras, spectrometers and a small lander to measure how Earth's gravity deforms the asteroid during the encounter.

"Apophis gives us a natural experiment we could never afford to build," said the mission's lead scientist. "Watching a planetary tide reshape an asteroid in real time will teach us more about rubble-pile bodies than a decade of remote observation."

The April 2029 passage will be visible to the naked eye across Europe, Africa and western Asia — the closest approach by an asteroid of this size in recorded history.

metadata.seoTitle: ESA approves Ramses mission to asteroid Apophis
metadata.keywords: ESA, Apophis, Ramses, asteroid, planetary defence

items.title: Ariane 6 Upper Stage Upgrade Promises Double Payload to Lunar Orbit
items.summary: ArianeGroup unveils the Astris kick stage for Ariane 6, targeting direct injections to lunar orbit and a European answer to the growing cislunar logistics market.
items.content:
ArianeGroup has presented the long-awaited Astris kick stage for the Ariane 6 launcher, an upgrade the company says will double the rocket's payload capacity to lunar transfer orbit and open a European route to the rapidly growing cislunar market.

The restartable stage, powered by the storable-propellant Berta engine, sits atop the existing upper stage and handles the final injection burn — freeing the core vehicle for heavier initial lifts. First flight is pencilled in for late 2027 with a navigation-satellite deployment.

European officials frame the upgrade as strategic autonomy: "If Europe wants a seat at the lunar table, it cannot rent the chair," an ESA transportation director told reporters at the Paris Air Show.

Analysts note the move positions Ariane 6 against a crowded field of commercial heavy-lift providers competing for Gateway resupply and lunar lander contracts into the 2030s.

metadata.seoTitle: Ariane 6 Astris kick stage doubles lunar payload
metadata.keywords: Ariane 6, ESA, Astris, lunar orbit, launcher

items.title: JUICE Probe Confirms Subsurface Ocean Signature During Second Ganymede Pass
items.summary: Data from ESA's JUICE spacecraft strengthens the case for a salty ocean beneath Ganymede's icy crust, with magnetometer readings matching decade-old predictions to within a few percent.
items.content:
ESA's Jupiter Icy Moons Explorer has returned magnetometer data from its second close pass of Ganymede that scientists say is the strongest evidence yet for a conductive, salty ocean tens of kilometres beneath the moon's frozen surface.

The induced magnetic field measured during the 400-kilometre flyby matched models of a subsurface ocean to within a few percent — a result the instrument team called "almost uncomfortably clean" at this week's European Planetary Science Congress.

Ganymede is the only moon in the Solar System that generates its own magnetic field, which makes separating the internal dynamo from the ocean's induced signal a delicate exercise. The new pass, timed to a different position in Jupiter's magnetospheric cycle, allowed the team to disentangle the two contributions.

JUICE will settle into orbit around Ganymede in 2034, the first time any spacecraft has orbited a moon other than our own.

metadata.seoTitle: JUICE confirms Ganymede subsurface ocean signal
metadata.keywords: JUICE, ESA, Ganymede, Jupiter, subsurface ocean
